subject: general
title: Some students are very advanced, others far behind — in the same class
query_text: Some students are very advanced, others far behind — in the same class

immediate_steps.title: Immediate Action
steps: Group students into 3 mixed-ability clusters of ~13 each — seat them together permanently for peer support
Prepare 3 versions of the same task: one with pictures/fewer steps (beginners), one standard, one with an extension challenge — same topic, different depth
Assign 'Learning Buddies' — one stronger student paired with one struggling student within each group, rotating monthly

classroom_activity.title: Classroom Activity — 'Same Question, My Way'
classroom_activity.activity: Write one open question on the board (e.g., 'How many ways can you make 20?'). Beginners use fingers/tally marks and write 2–3 ways. Mid-level students write 5+ ways using addition/subtraction. Advanced students find patterns and write a rule. All share answers — class sees that everyone contributed something valid. No one feels left out.
classroom_activity.materials: Blackboard, chalk, notebooks, pencils — nothing else needed

inclusion_strategy.title: Inclusion Strategy
inclusion_strategy.strategy: Use TaRL grouping: briefly assess where each child actually is (not their grade level) using a simple oral or written task. Then teach struggling students foundational concepts in a small group for 10 minutes while advanced students work independently on extension tasks. Rotate your attention — you are not ignoring anyone.

reflection_prompt.title: Reflection
reflection_prompt.question: After trying mixed-ability grouping this week, which student surprised you — either by helping others more than expected, or by understanding something you thought they couldn't?

source_citation.title: Source
source_citation.reference: TaRL (Teaching at the Right Level) Methodology — Pratham/J-PAL India; NEP 2020 Section 4.6 — Equitable and Inclusive Education; NCERT Pedagogical Guidelines on differentiated instruction
